item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright so back to back mathematics based scips may be partially to blame for my dislike of this one but I also take issue with the idea of using it on D-class personnel. This is a dangerous memetic cognitohazard and O5 is fine with cross contaminating what could reasonably be expected to be scips that require high levels of intellect. Yeah that couldn't possibly blow up in the Foundations face. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. Apart from not being run of the mill or overly extravagant I sort of think I have gotten the feeling described within where I am thinking or simulating something obsessively and feel overloaded by trying to keep track of all the moving parts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember this being one of the first SCPs I read. It’s interesting, unique, and not too hard to wrap your head around. This was probably one of the ones that got me hooked on the site. Great work.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addendum: Request to use SCP-1314-A to increase logical and spacial-temporal reasoning skills of D-class personnel for use in unrelated experiments. –Dr. █████████.

Exactly what I was thinking while reading this, lol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely loved this one, especially as I started trying to picture the universe in my head as I read. This one would totally destroy me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. +1

Am I to understand, then, that the simulated universe is deterministic in nature?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And besides, one time unit would take more or less time to calculate based on how many different things are happening in that time unit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, that's fair enough. I was under the assumption that "time units" was used for the progression in our universe.

Though I'm agreed with you there, since there isn't any other indication of the length of time involved to actually simulate it.

EDIT: in fact, assuming time progresses the same way, and since we can measure the theoretical universe's time in such a way as to have a quantifiable unit, surely we could express it in terms of a known unit? Like "the simulation of one time unit for an average subject takes roughly 3.4 seconds".

…although that would be really dull and unnecessary, so just a rough overall estimate of the time between the start of the calculations and the person dying would probably be best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a simulation of a universe unrelated to ours, with entirely different contents and laws of physics. There's no particular reason that a time unit in the simulation would correspond to anything we have in this universe.

That being said, it would be nice to know how long this whole process usualy takes to play out, in our universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
time units

What unit is this referring to? Milliseconds, seconds, minutes, hours, days, months and centuries are all technically units used to measure time, so "time units" doesn't really give a frame for the amount of time that's actually passed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this is related to SCP-1190 somehow. If you could run 1314 for long enough, on hardware that was sufficiently powerful, what would you get?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much with the digging of this and with the upvoting andall like that there. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP has fascinated me since I first read it. I would love to see some associated experimental logs for it. The rules for this universe seem just complex enough to embed difficult computational problems in, so I'd love to know what happens in the following experiments:

A mathematics/computer science researcher, having been exposed to -A, is allowed to concoct his own set of initial conditions. Another researcher, not exposed to -A, compresses and encodes these initial conditions into a document, copying in the relevant memetic triggers from -B. Resulting document and -A are exposed to a D-Class to see what happens.

1) The initial conditions describe an empty universe, or a universe with one object.
2) The initial conditions describe a large number of objects in stable orbits that never collide, or a universe which spatially expands faster than any of the objects in it are traveling.
3) The initial conditions describe a universe which encodes an NP-complete problem instance (a TSP or SAT instance, perhaps) just large enough that a person would usually be unable to solve it, but not too hard to solve by computer.
4) The initial conditions describe a universe which encodes an NP-complete problem instance which does not require a large number of objects to encode, but still entails such a large number of solution possibilities that a computer would typically take a long time to solve it.

The kind of results I would find interesting is whether human creativity is applied toward the memetic computational task in order to make processing it more efficient. For instance, I would expect the empty/stable universes to be considered "solved" as soon as the person recognizes that they do not change or continually repeat, and therefore these subjects will return to normal activity with improved mathematical intelligence. I would expect the expanding universe to perhaps reach a similar state, although if it does not repeat, it might continually be running in the back of the subject's mind, though not requiring an overwhelming amount of concentration or much interrupting their life. I don't know what to expect from the NP-complete problem tests, but perhaps the human creativity in the loop could engineer a way to solve those problems more quickly than a computer could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would assume that "standard memetic obsession agent" is just a descriptor of the effects, and the Foundation would have a division dedicated to the classification of memetic effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A standard memetic agent? Are they standardized now? Is there a standards body that makes rules to certify new memetic agents?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a huge deal, but the containment procedures bug me. Why was it okay to destory -A, but -B must be saved? And why does -B need to be scanned every month? Nothing in the description mentions it changing, so one scan should be sufficient.

Other than that I like it. Death by intellectual preocupation, don't think that's been done before.

Side-note: I'm in a minority here, but I don't see a problem with cross-contamination. Yes, there are valid arguement in-universe for why it would be haevily discouraged. But in terms of the wiki, it makes for much more interesting fiction. For something like this, where the effect is narrowly defined and the threat of poorly understood side-effects is minimal, I think the O5s wouldn't have a problem using the available asset to its full potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely like the concept (and have upvoted) - expecially the fact that the simulated universe is a radically simplified version of our own one designed to run (for a while) on human hardware. That said, I second (third?) thamuzz and TedlyAnderson's comments about gauging the rate at which the subjects' SRI and LRI rises. I stumbled over the following:

they will repeatedly review SCP-1314-A, with the effect of raising their SRI by 0.3 and LRI by 0.1 for each minute of exposure until reaching 82 and 93 respectively.

This seems oddly focused on the subject and makes it feel like a target the subject is setting for themselves rather than a passive effect caused by SCP-1314-A. In combination with TedlyAnderson's wording, I'd suggest something like:

If the subject does not meet these conditions, they will experience a compulsion to repeatedly review SCP-1314-A. Exposure has been shown to progressively raise the subject's SRI and LRI by an average of 3 units per hour until they reach the minimum thresholds for obsession with SCP-1314-B.

I also presume that exposure to SCP-1314-A does not give subjects any guidance as to the location of SCP-1314-B - that is, they know they're looking for an code somewhere but don't know where? They have to have seen SCP-1314-A before for SCP-1314-B to work but the reverse doesn't seem to be true. Personally I'd find it more satisfying if they were inspired to recreate the equations, although this would obviously prove a rather more challenging prospect for containment and might not be what you're looking for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Learn something new every day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, it is a common misconception that certain parts of the brain are specifically and only devoted a certain task. While certain parts, such as the brain stem, have very specific functions, much of the brain shares the work, while the parts we consider the main functional area, such as Broca's and Wernicke's areas for speech, are actually more of the director, pulling processing power from other parts of the brain.

This is called distributed processing, and explains why damage to one part of the brain does not always cause loss of function, as other parts "pick up the slack". We are much more complex than anyone knows. My opinion is that it is perfectly reasonable for this SCP to "overwrite" various functions of the brain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While hard to follow, I think the process of how the effects occur is clear enough that the overall effect is understandable. Really, this is a "crazy to death" memetic SCP done right: getting that process explanation makes it all work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really thought I was going to hate this when I saw the name, but I was wrong. Nice job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was of the impression that different regions of the brain had vastly different purposes

It's true, but the brain is also highly adaptable; damage or trauma to one area can result in another area "taking up the slack", so to speak. There are still parts of the brain that won't give up their primary functions (like, say, the pituitary gland), but for the most part the brain is capable of using its resources very flexibly. For example, I recall a case study described in Oliver Sacks' The Man Who Mistook His Wife For A Hat in which a patient had a massive tumor in his head that squeezed his brain's volume to something like 1/4 its usual size; the remaining mass had to significantly adjust its processing to allow him motor and language skills, and while they were jerky and obviously impaired, he still functioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My knowledge of the brain is fairly limited, but this bothered me a great deal as well. I was of the impression that different regions of the brain had vastly different purposes, to the extent that trying to use your speech centers as memory makes about as much sense as trying to use your bones as skin.

Also, my headcanon misfired hard over that addendum. Cross contamination considered harmful. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is just on the verge of venturing into a territory that I know nothing about, but I have one issue.

When the impact event occurs, the subject will show distress, as they need to mentally keep track of hundreds of thousands of objects simultaneously. After this point, subjects begin overwriting their previous memories to keep track of the objects. Once this space is expended, the effect spreads into more critical parts of the brain. No subject has remained capable of speech after 30,300 time units. Eventually, the subject will overwrite their involuntary muscle control, resulting in death by either asphyxiation or cardiac arrest.

subjects begin overwriting their previous memories to keep track of the objects.

I have a problem with this. Correct me if I'm wrong, but as far as I know, the brain doesn't work like a computer hard drive. Certain parts are devoted to specific tasks, and those parts would serve no purpose in mentally calculating the position of hypothetical objects. I'm not a neuroscientist; I just believe that out brain processes differently than a RAM chip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple of minor nitpicks: first, I think the first few paragraphs of the description go into a bit too much pedantic detail. My eyes were starting to glaze over by the time I got to the interesting parts. It doesn't need a big edit, just a bit of streamlining.

Second, the plural of polyhedron is polyhedra.

Upvoted anyway, I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree—the extremely precise measurement of increase of spatial-reasoning skills (precisely 0.1 per minute?) is jarring. An approximation would be more realistic (i.e. "test subjects showed an average of 3 [units] increase per hour of reading 1314-A" or something similar). Other than that, I like it quite a bit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1314-B is essentially harmless on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I imagine the "awareness" came from the fact that she's a Foundation Agent and understands memetic/info/cognitohazards, and how to recognize them even when under their effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sigma!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll start with my one real issue with this article:

The original copy of SCP-1314-B is to be stored in a clearly marked A4 Manilla envelope kept in Dr. ██████’s office.

I know that a lot of the older articles have this kind of containment, but it feels weird when considered against current standards. I would think that the Foundation would want tighter control over a potentially lethal SCP that can easily be abused. I would suggest putting it in a locked container with a security log or something.

Now, that being said, I really like the concept. My first read-through, I was afraid it was going to be something that makes you smarter for no reason, but the progressive side effects of simulating this model on it victims was pretty cool. Your tone is also good and the article is well-written. I hesitate to call attention to a writer's experience, since I fervently believe that all articles on the website should be judged on their merits alone, but this is a good article in general, and an excellent first article.

Well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gaining that level of logical reasoning skills is a part of 1314-A's effect. The effect of 1314-B can't work until you've been exposed to the prerequisite 1314-A, which requires a prerequisite Logical Reasoning Index of 93. This would equate to the level of competence to perform the calculations involved, but in hindsight I guess that isn't overly clear. I'll see if I can make that a bit clearer. As for comprehension of the symbols, I just intended to have a table of symbols be a part of 1314-A, but memetic comprehension is a better idea.

As for the equations, I just grabbed an image from Google and posted some black boxes over a few of them. Looking at what they actually are shows that they're probability equations, which don't have any place in a deterministic simulation at all! I'll try and rectify this in the morning. Thanks for pointing this out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right you are, fixed. Thanks for pointing that out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A 450k BMP file? Needs to be properly encoded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first problem I have with this is with the use of objective intelligence measurements. It's possible that the Foundation has some more advanced metrics to work with, but it seriously hurts my suspension of disbelief.
Then there's the bit about people below the threshold having their intelligence boosted to the threshold in an objective, quantifiable way (as the result of a memetic attack, no less), that kicks my suspension of disbelief in the balls and runs away with its wallet.
The bit at the end where O5 approves the use of an SCP object as a means to improve Class-D: no, bad author! No upvote! O5 wants to avoid cross contamination, not encourage it.

Beyond that, there are some tone issues. A significant portion of the article is devoted to describing the simulated universe, which feels a bit blunt and straightforward as far as SCPs go.
In the end, it's a memetic DoS attack that kills you.

All that said, I would like to point out that it does show promise as a first article.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do believe the term is "polyhedrons" rather than "polygons".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. A question:

in the later stages of infection, performing crosswords and mental exercises with the intent of delaying her calculations

Is the intention here that infected are aware of what's going on with them and attempt to hinder their ability to do the simulation? If so, at what stage does this awareness manifests, from the beginning or only when the simulation start to rewrite their memories? Would a math professor attempting to solve a difficult unrelated math problem be more successful at temporally halting the simulation?

EDIT: .bmp images are EVIL, especially for math formulas. This is exactly the thing .png was invented for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From the context I am pretty sure that Σ is used as a covariance matrix and not as a summation operator here. I don't know if the current notation is correct for a covariance matrix, however.

item.title: Re: New SCP
item.content:encoded: Well, I don't. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would think that being able to memorize and process at least 100 different equations on dozens of different objects, over and over again, without using paper or a calculator, would take a mathematical idiot-savant. If gaining that level of mathematical skill was part of the anomalous memetic effect, fine, but it should be noted. Also, a person who doesn't know what the various mathematical symbols mean shouldn't be affected, unless the anomalous memetic effect is granting knowledge of what they mean.

Finally, the use of the Σ notation in the equations is wrong. If you have something like "Σ x", then under the Σ there should be "x=[starting value]", and above it be "[ending value]".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The danger being directly related to the powerful creative and imaginative potential in our brains, running absolutely haywire. Death by DoS. Dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. Original idea (at least to me), and an interesting way of mind-wiping someone. Very well written, and wonderful pacing.

item.title: New SCP
item.content:encoded: So here's my first SCP. I hope you don't hate it.
